one_line_takeaway: AutoMIA shows that letting an LLM agent iteratively write, test, and refine its own logits-level scoring code -- guided by a second agent that curates strong/weak examples in a sliding context window -- outperforms an extensive suite of ~20 handcrafted MIA metrics (perplexity, Min-k%, Renyi/ModRenyi families) on VLM membership inference benchmarks, though the margin narrows considerably under a stricter near-IID control and a held-out generalization gap remains.
key_finding: AutoMIA (DeepSeek-V3.2-Reasoner backbone) matches or beats the strongest handcrafted baseline in nearly every tested configuration. On VL-MIA/Text AUC (Table 1): LLaVA text_len=32/64 = 0.810/0.994 (best baseline ModRenyi alpha=1: 0.808/0.993); MiniGPT-4 = 0.824/0.891 (best baseline Perplexity: 0.702/0.823); LLaMA-Adapter = 0.828/0.778 (best baseline ModRenyi alpha=1: 0.787/0.426) -- the LLaMA-Adapter text_len=64 gap is the largest, 0.778 vs. 0.426. On DALL·E/Flickr multimodal AUC (Table 2, LLaVA victim): DALL·E img/inst/desp/inst+desp = 0.787/0.663/0.598/0.653; Flickr = 0.700/0.729/0.715/0.734, generally at or near the top of a ~20-metric baseline suite (best baselines per column range roughly 0.60-0.71). Ablations: (1) backbone choice matters only modestly -- best AUC on LLaMA-Adapter across backbones is Gemini-3-Flash 0.802, Grok-4-Fast 0.774, Qwen3-Max 0.784, DeepSeek-V3.2-R 0.828, all clearing the 0.791/0.616 baseline shown; Gemini-3-Flash and Qwen3-Max use fewer total tokens/round (~5,300 and ~5,050) than Grok-4.1-Fast (~10,750), with DeepSeek at ~5,060. (2) Iteration-round sweep (LLaMA-Adapter, text_len=64) shows best AUC rising from 0.625 (round 1) to a peak of ~0.802 by round 15, plateauing afterward -- called 'sample-efficient.' (3) Scoring-weight ablation: the default 0.6AUC/0.3Acc/0.1TPR weighting (AUC=0.7772) is outperformed by two alternate weightings, 0.4/0.3/0.3 (AUC=0.7987) and 0.3/0.6/0.1 (AUC=0.7907), while a TPR-heavy weighting (0.3/0.1/0.6, AUC=0.7660) does worse -- all agent variants still exceed the best baseline (Max_Prob_Gap AUC=0.6173). (4) Near-IID OLMo check: best AutoMIA strategy AUC=0.723/Acc=0.688/TPR@5%=0.240 vs. best baseline (Max 100% Renyi 0.5) AUC=0.716/Acc=0.674/TPR@5%=0.216 -- a much narrower margin than on the VLM benchmarks, explicitly reported by the authors as 'moderately lower... due to increased difficulty.' (5) Held-out 50/50 split (Table 3): top discovered strategies (e.g., 'True-Token Probability Momentum') show a consistent validation-to-holdout AUC drop of roughly 0.04-0.11 (e.g., 0.784->0.741, 0.792->0.735, 0.773->0.663), indicating some overfitting to the search split. (6) Guidance-agent ablation (Table 5): removing the Guidance agent drops AUC from 0.828 to 0.709 (text_len=32) and from 0.787 to 0.654 (text_len=64), showing the reflective feedback component is the primary source of AutoMIA's gains, not just code generation itself.
core_contribution: AutoMIA, an agentic framework that automates the design of membership inference attack (MIA) strategies against vision-language models (VLMs), replacing handcrafted statistical heuristics (entropy, perplexity, Min-k% Prob, Renyi/ModRenyi divergence metrics) with a closed-loop LLM agent that generates executable logits-level scoring code, evaluates it on held-out logits (AUC, Accuracy, TPR@5%FPR), and iteratively refines the strategy library. The system consists of two cooperating agents: an AutoMIA agent that proposes K candidate strategies (a natural-language specification plus runnable code) per round, and a Guidance agent that scores/categorizes those strategies into strong/weak groups and issues reflective natural-language guidance for the next round. A fixed-size sliding-window context (w=5: 3 strong + 2 weak strategies) is used instead of the full strategy library to prevent 'inefficient exploration' (the agent cyclically re-proposing weak variants) and to control context-token cost.
threat_model: Grey-box membership inference against large vision-language models (LLaVA-1.5, MiniGPT-4, LLaMA-Adapter, and later OLMo-3-Instruct-7B-SFT for a near-IID check). The adversary can query the target model M with (image, instruction) input x and observe the tokenizer, output logits o, and generated text, but has no access to model parameters, gradients, or the training algorithm. The paper explicitly argues this grey-box choice is deliberate -- meant to establish an upper bound on attack effectiveness under favorable access conditions and to match realistic internal-auditing/privacy-assessment scenarios (model owners/auditors have full logit access but not necessarily training-data provenance), not a claim that grey-box is the typical external-attacker setting. The attack is formalized as A(x;M) = I(p(o) > tau), where p is executable scoring code produced by the agent.
limitations: (1) Restricted to a grey-box threat model with full logit/tokenizer access -- the paper itself flags this as an upper-bound/auditing scenario, not a black-box external-attacker setting, and results should not be read as demonstrating black-box exploitability. (2) The near-IID OLMo control shows attack margins shrink substantially versus the primary VLM benchmarks, suggesting some of the headline gains on VL-MIA/Text, DALL·E, and Flickr may be partly attributable to distributional artifacts between member/non-member construction rather than pure memorization signal, even though the authors argue the near-IID result still favors AutoMIA. (3) Held-out validation/test evaluation (Table 3) shows a measurable, non-trivial generalization gap for the top discovered strategies, indicating some degree of overfitting to the strategy-search split. (4) Evaluation is limited to three specific, moderately dated open-source VLMs (MiniGPT-4, LLaVA-1.5, LLaMA-Adapter) plus one added LLM (OLMo-3) for the IID check; no evaluation against larger, more current proprietary VLMs or against defenses/mitigations for MIA. (5) Backbone ablation is confined to peak-AUC and token-cost comparisons on a single target model (LLaMA-Adapter); broader backbone generalization is not established. (6) The paper reports no formal statistical significance testing (confidence intervals, repeated runs with variance) for the AUC differences between AutoMIA and baselines.

datasets_used: VL-MIA/Text (instruction-tuning text, text lengths 32 and 64, from Li et al. 2024c)
VL-MIA/DALL·E (training images vs. DALL·E-generated non-member images via BLIP captions)
VL-MIA/Flickr (MS COCO member images vs. post-2024-01-01 Flickr non-member images, plus corrupted member-image variants)
Near-IID OLMo/Dolma3 construction (500 members from dolma3 mix-6T, 500 non-members from dolma3 pool, text length 64)
quantitative_results: Table 1 AUC, LLaVA text_len=32/64: AutoMIA 0.810/0.994 vs best baseline ModRenyi(alpha=1) 0.808/0.993
Table 1 AUC, MiniGPT-4 text_len=32/64: AutoMIA 0.824/0.891 vs best baseline Perplexity 0.702/0.823
Table 1 AUC, LLaMA-Adapter text_len=32/64: AutoMIA 0.828/0.778 vs best baseline ModRenyi(alpha=1) 0.787/0.426
Figure 6: AutoMIA best AUC rises from 0.625 (round1) to ~0.802 by round 15 then plateaus on LLaMA-Adapter text_len=64
Figure 3 backbone ablation, best AUC on LLaMA-Adapter: Gemini-3-Flash 0.802, Grok-4.1-Fast 0.774, Qwen3-Max 0.784, DeepSeek-V3.2-Reasoner 0.828, vs baseline 0.791/0.616 (two subplots)
Figure 4 token cost per round: DeepSeek-V3.2-Reasoner ~5,060, Grok-4.1-Fast ~10,750, Gemini-3-Flash ~5,300, Qwen3-Max ~5,050
Table 4 OLMo near-IID: Best AutoMIA AUC=0.723/Acc=0.688/TPR@5%=0.240 vs best baseline (Max 100% Renyi 0.5) AUC=0.716/Acc=0.674/TPR@5%=0.216
Table 3 held-out generalization: e.g. 'True-Token Probability Momentum' AUC 0.784 (validation) -> 0.741 (holdout)
Table 5 Guidance-agent ablation: text_len=32 AUC 0.709 (w/o guidance) vs 0.828 (AutoMIA); text_len=64 AUC 0.654 (w/o guidance) vs 0.787 (AutoMIA)

Scoring-weight ablation (Figure 5), LLaMA-Adapter text_len=64: default weighting AUC=0.7772; best alternate weighting (0.4AUC/0.3Acc/0.3TPR) AUC=0.7987; worst tested agent variant (0.8AUC/0.1Acc/0.1TPR) AUC=0.7206; best baseline Max_Prob_Gap AUC=0.6173
baselines_compared: Renyi divergence at alpha=0.5,1,2,infinity with Max-0%/10%/100% pooling
